skill.name: tmux
skill.description: Manage isolated tmux sessions for background commands, interactive shells, and development services.
skill.summary: Control persistent tmux sessions for long-running terminal work.
skill.author: Dwsy

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, environment, and blocker patterns. Review found no prompt injection, exfiltration, or confirmed malicious intent; many documentation, hidden-path, weak-crypto, and keylogger hits are false positives. The skill remains medium risk because it intentionally starts shell commands in tmux, stores session metadata under temporary paths, and can capture or print terminal output.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Command Execution by Design
medium_findings.description: The skill creates tmux sessions and sends the requested command to a shell. This is core functionality, but it can execute arbitrary commands if callers pass untrusted input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The implementation directly accepts a command string and sends it into a tmux shell. The behavior is intentional and documented, so the risk is operational rather than hidden malware.

medium_findings.title: Terminal Output and Command Metadata Exposure
medium_findings.description: The skill stores session metadata and can capture pane output. Commands or terminal text may contain credentials, tokens, build secrets, or private logs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code persists session records and prints captured pane text on timeout. This is expected for a tmux helper, but it can disclose sensitive terminal output.

medium_findings.title: Predictable Temporary Socket and Store Paths
medium_findings.description: Default sockets and session state are placed under temporary directories. Multi-user systems should review permissions before storing sensitive commands or output there.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The default paths are clearly defined under /tmp or TMPDIR. This is common for tmux sockets, but permissions and shared-host exposure need review.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Is Limited to Configuration
low_findings.description: The environment variable findings are configuration reads for temporary directory and socket location. No evidence found that environment secrets are transmitted or logged.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced variables select paths only. I did not find evidence of secret harvesting or network transmission.

low_findings.title: Hidden Home Directory References Are Documentation Paths
low_findings.description: The hidden-file findings mostly refer to example install paths under a hidden home directory. They are not evidence of covert file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are user-facing examples showing how to run the skill. The implementation does not read hidden home files at these locations.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Terms Are Benign tmux Terminology
low_findings.description: The keylogger and weak-crypto blocker hits are false positives from tmux key sending, the word includes, and documentation text. No evidence found of credential capture or cryptographic misuse.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged terms appear in normal tmux control and documentation contexts. I did not find persistence, stealth capture, or cryptographic code tied to these hits.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Shell Command Dispatch
dangerous_patterns.description: The create command accepts caller-provided command text and runs it inside a tmux-managed shell. Use only with trusted commands and trusted prompt inputs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code path is direct and documented. Bun template escaping protects the tmux invocation, but the final command is deliberately executed inside the spawned shell.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Supplied Regex Against Captured Terminal Text
dangerous_patterns.description: The wait helpers accept caller-provided patterns and evaluate them against captured pane text. Complex regex patterns may cause slow matching on large output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The regex behavior is explicit and useful, but it can be expensive with untrusted patterns. The shell script offers fixed-string matching, which reduces the risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Captured Pane Output Printed on Timeout
dangerous_patterns.description: The wait script prints the last captured pane text when a pattern is not found. This can expose secrets that appeared in terminal output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Both implementations emit captured terminal content during failure handling. This is helpful for debugging but can disclose sensitive command output.

content.value_statement: Long-running terminal work is hard to monitor from one-off commands. This skill creates, tracks, captures, and cleans up isolated tmux sessions for agents and developers.
actual_capabilities: Create named tmux sessions for tasks, services, and agent workflows.
Send literal keystrokes to an existing tmux pane.
Capture recent pane output for monitoring or debugging.
List, sync, attach to, and kill tracked sessions.
Store session metadata so work can be recovered later.
Poll tmux output until expected text appears or a timeout expires.
limitations: Requires tmux, Bun, and a Unix-like shell environment.
Runs commands with the current user permissions.
Does not sandbox commands or validate command safety.
Captured output may include sensitive terminal text.

use_cases.title: Monitor Long Builds
use_cases.description: Run compile, test, or migration jobs in tmux and inspect progress without blocking the main agent session.

use_cases.title: Manage Development Services
use_cases.description: Start local servers in persistent sessions, capture logs, and stop services when work is complete.

use_cases.title: Automate Interactive CLIs
use_cases.description: Control REPLs and terminal tools by sending keys, waiting for prompts, and collecting output.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Simple Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the tmux skill to start a task named build that runs my project build command. Show me how to monitor it.

prompt_templates.title: Check Task Progress
prompt_templates.prompt: List my tracked tmux sessions, find the active build or test session, and capture the latest output.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Local Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a service session for my development server, wait until the ready message appears, then provide the attach and stop commands.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Interactive Debugging
prompt_templates.prompt: Start an interactive Python session in tmux, wait for the prompt, send a few diagnostic commands, capture the output, and clean up afterward.

output_examples.input: Start a long-running test job and tell me how to watch it.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a named tmux task, reports the session identifier, and gives monitor, capture, and cleanup instructions.

output_examples.input: Check whether my dev server is ready.
output_examples.output: The assistant captures recent session output, searches for the ready message, and summarizes the service status.

output_examples.input: Clean up stale tmux sessions from yesterday.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists old tracked sessions, removes the requested ones, and reports how many sessions were closed.

best_practices: Use clear session names that describe the task or service.
Avoid placing secrets in commands or terminal output when capture is enabled.
Kill or clean up sessions when work is complete.
anti_patterns: Do not run commands from untrusted prompt text without review.
Do not use captured pane output as a secure secret store.
Do not leave long-running services active after they are no longer needed.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It lets an agent create, monitor, control, and clean up tmux sessions for terminal work.

faq.question: Does it install tmux?
faq.answer: No. tmux must already be installed on the machine.

faq.question: Can it run arbitrary commands?
faq.answer: Yes. That is the main capability, so commands should come from trusted users or reviewed workflows.

faq.question: Where does it store session state?
faq.answer: By default, it stores session metadata under the temporary directory configured for the environment.

faq.question: Can it manage interactive tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It can send keys to a pane and wait for prompt text from tools such as REPLs.

faq.question: Is it safe for shared machines?
faq.answer: Use caution. Review temporary directory permissions and avoid commands or output that contain secrets.
